Studying cost-effective ways to ensure crop biodiversity and increase access to nutritious foods through partnerships with tribal communities and developing a data sovereignty model. column466: Advancing the Design and Fabrication of Biopolymer-based Nanocomposites for Sustainable Food Packaging
column467:
The project will address research areas of reducing and redirecting food loss/waste and improving processing and packaging technologies. Specifically, we will demonstrate the effective design and fabrication of biopolymer-based nanocomposites for effective and sustainable food packaging, by integrating nanotechnological processing technology, multiscale computational modeling, and artificial intelligence.

Key topics or areas of research to be covered in the nominee’s program (50-word limit)
The project will address research area of improving processing and packaging technologies and contributing to advancing UN Sustainable Development goals. Specifically, we will demonstrate the effective design and fabrication of biopolymer-based nanocomposites for effective and sustainable food packaging, by integrating nanotechnological processing technology, multiscale computational modeling, and artificial intelligence.

column455:
P.I.'s should be given more time to become aquainted with new reporting requirements. When the upmetrics survey landed in my inbox, it came without context from FFAR and there were a few weeks of ambiquity for me about which reporting was required - upmetrics or the one on the grants management page. The emails clarifying these changes came later.
 
 Reporting documents should also be organized so that P.I.'s don't need to spend extensive time finding documents. For example you reference the FFAR Indicator Handbook in q.25 but this handbook has never been previously referenced or socialized with our team. Our indicators are not congruent with the categories noted above and we will have to translate across the two. 
 
 I believe in monitoring and evaluation and built my own framework that we have been using but the categories don't match between my indicators and what is in q.25.
 
 Looking through the indicator handbook it is clear that q.25 does not capture social science research products. Which is problematic since questions are products are not clearly articulated in the annual report prompts. We have done a lot of work and made a lot of headway but it is difficult to capture that progress and implementation across the new reporting structures.

column455:
The FFAR Indicator Handbook was a bit ambiguous for community-engaged research projects that do not follow traditional academic pathways. For example, Indicator #2 distinguishes between "extension publications" and "other" end-user focused articles, but it was unclear how to categorize resources like a Resource Map or a Strategic Roadmap developed specifically for philanthropic funders. These products are end-user focused but do not fit neatly into the provided categories.

Several indicators are oriented toward traditional scientific research outputs‚Äîpeer-reviewed publications, IP rights, licensing strategies, and research trainees‚Äîwhich may not align with the outputs of community-engaged or action-oriented research projects. Our project produced value through relationship-building, capacity development, and practical resources for underserved communities, yet many of these contributions are difficult to capture in the current indicator framework. Consider adding indicators that measure community partnerships established or capacity built among stakeholder groups.

The distinction between "practice or product-related events" (Indicator #8) and "scientific-focused events" (Indicator #9) was unclear for workshops that served multiple purposes. Our training workshops were designed to share research findings with farmers (practice-related) but also included discussions of systemic barriers and policy implications that could be considered scientific.

Indicator #3 asks for instances where researchers were referenced in end-user focused publications. This is difficult to track because there is no systematic way to monitor when others reference our work.

For Indicator #14, determining the correct stage of research, development, and deployment for knowledge products required interpretation. Our guides and resources are published and being used by producers and funders, which suggests Stage 7 (validated and ready for introduction) or possibly Stage 8 (undergoing user validation). However, we lack data on adoption rates or user feedback to definitively place products in later stages.

I find the "number of innovation and knowledge products" to be a little abstract. Some of the examples in the handbook are: 

‚Ä¢ research concerning the effectiveness of policy options (policy research) 
‚Ä¢ research on the socio-behavioral, socioeconomic or sociopolitical factors that influence decision-making 

It's difficult to summarize "research" as a "number of knowledge products". It's not clear what would constitute one product versus two products for one a category focused on research. If the research is focused on the same general topic, but two publications are produced, is this then two products? I completely recognize the challenges associated with monitoring and evaluating research, and I appreciate this attempt to do these things more quantitatively, but the conversion of a research project into something can be counted isn't always straightforward. Perhaps more clarification on how to do this would be helpful.

column456:
Minimal symbiotic genome and design rules for symbiotic effectiveness (Stage 2): We defined a minimal symbiotic gene set sufficient for nodulation and nitrogen fixation in two model rhizobia and identified additional metabolic determinants required for effective nitrogen fixation. This knowledge product establishes a refined genetic blueprint for symbiosis and is intended to guide future engineering of nitrogen-fixing microbes by clarifying which components are necessary and which are missing from canonical models.
 
 Design rules for genetic compatibility and conflict in symbiosis engineering (Stage 2): We identified key constraints in engineered symbiosis, including negative interactions between redundant gene sets and the importance of regulatory compatibility. This knowledge product defines principles for combining symbiotic elements and is intended to improve future engineering strategies by avoiding detrimental genetic interactions.
 
 Framework for symbiotic competitiveness across the plant lifecycle (Stage 3): We developed an experimental and analytical framework to quantify rhizobium competitiveness across multiple stages of the plant-associated lifecycle, including rhizosphere colonization, nodule occupancy, and persistence. This framework is intended to identify strains and genetic determinants that contribute to competitive success, supporting improved selection and engineering of symbiotic microbes.
 
 Modular synthetic genomics platform for symbiosis engineering (Stage 3): We developed a synthetic genomics pipeline for iterative assembly and testing of symbiotic gene clusters in rhizobial backgrounds. This platform enables systematic evaluation of gene modules and is intended to support bottom-up engineering of symbiosis by allowing controlled addition and testing of candidate determinants.
 
 Hybrid/top-down strategy for engineering new-to-nature symbiosis (Stage 3): We demonstrated that combining symbiotic plasmids from different rhizobia can enable functional nitrogen fixation in a non-native host. This approach provides a complementary strategy to bottom-up engineering and is intended to identify functional combinations of symbiotic elements and guide reconstruction of symbiosis in new host systems.
 
 Microbiome-derived chassis discovery pipeline (Stage 3): We developed an integrated pipeline combining culturomics and sequencing to identify plant-associated microbes with potential for symbiosis engineering. This pipeline is intended to generate candidate microbial hosts that are naturally adapted to crop environments and suitable for downstream engineering.
 
 Transfer of minimal symbiotic programs into cereal-associated microbes (Stage 3): We demonstrated initial transfer of symbiosis gene sets into cereal-associated microbial isolates and evaluated early phenotypic responses. This work represents an early step toward extending symbiosis engineering into crop-relevant microbiomes and is intended to inform future development of engineered microbial systems for cereals.
 
 VECTOR platform for microbial engineering (Stage 4): We developed the VECTOR platform, a versatile plasmid-based system enabling high-throughput genetic engineering across diverse plant-associated microbes. This platform supports rapid testing of gene expression and symbiotic function in multiple microbial chassis and has contributed to downstream translational efforts, including formation of a spin-off Frontier Bioforge although the unviersity elected not to pursue a patent for this IP. It is intended to accelerate development and deployment of engineered microbial technologies.

column455: For INDICATOR #14, the appropriate numbers depend strongly on how each Innovation Stage is interpreted. Stage‚ÄØ1 could reflect the full set of compounds tested in the primary screen, Stage‚ÄØ2 could reflect the compounds confirmed after the primary and confirmation screens, and Stage‚ÄØ3 could reflect those undergoing secondary validation assays. To keep our reporting consistent and meaningful, we chose to report: (1) the number of confirmed compounds from the primary screen for Stage‚ÄØ1, (2) the number of compounds retained after multiple secondary screens for Stage‚ÄØ2, and (3) the same number for Stage‚ÄØ3, as these compounds continue to undergo further secondary testing and validation in a relevant (soil) environment. This approach provides a clear and realistic picture of the discovery funnel.
column456: For Indicator #14, we entered values for Stages 1‚Äì3 based on the progression of compounds through our discovery pipeline. For Stage 1, we reported the 30 confirmed hit compounds identified from the primary screen, as these represent potential solution pathways. For Stage 2, we reported the 5 compounds that consistently showed strong soil efficacy across multiple secondary assays (1 lead compound and 4 active structural analogues), as these validated our solution hypothesis. For Stage 3, we reported the same 5 compounds, since these candidates are now undergoing further controlled laboratory testing‚Äîincluding stability, dose‚Äëresponse, and soil‚Äëtype interaction studies‚Äîto refine and validate their potential as biological nitrification inhibitors.

column456:
Stage 1:	We have heard consensus among our members and broader community around the need for an open, standardized benchmark dataset for agricultural soil and management data. We have likewise heard repeated need for a supportive data-to-model infrastructure that lowers the cost and increases standardization and transparency for how data are represented in models. We are in the design phase for each of these goals, but exact solutions are not yet determined. 
Stage 8: 	Our web platform and model API are very newly deployed and still in early testing phases. We are offering access to consortium members first to determine usage and cost before expanding access to the broader public. Simiarly, our R package that supports DayCent use is public on GitHub and we have shared it via word-of-mouth, but it has not been formally published in a publication or on CRAN and does not yet have wide usership. 
Stage 10: The Consortium offers licensed access to modeling software (DayCent & MEMS model executables). DayCent in particular is used widely by entities in the voluntary carbon market, companies doing Scope 3 reporting, and US and international researchers and government agencies. The Consortium structure allows us to share this technology widely, and manage and track access to different models and model versions.

column456:
Stage 1: Potential solution pathways identified - We identified that most urban farmers willing to establish edible resilient tree orchards have little to no access to robust information on crop tolerances for future climates. 

Stage 2: Solution hypothesis confirmed - We developed a big-data-based tree crop selection online tool that will be accessible to all urban farmers across the US, comprising people farming in some 2,000 community gardens, plus private residents. 

Stage 3: Undergoing lab/controlled research - Our tool is being Beta-tested for usability by a range of experts and stakeholders with a subset of 100 crops. The final tool will include climate suitability, edibility and nutrition data for over 500 edible tree crops.

Stage 8 Market/user validation test secured and being executed - Our tool is being Beta-tested for usability by a range of experts and stakeholders with a subset of 100 crops. This tool will be open access and we are currently compiling the most complete list of stakeholders across the US thanks for our professional networks and AI tools.

column456:
Stage 1: Potential solution pathways identified: The challenge is that although we know perennial plants grown in mixtures provide significantly improved soil health relative to annual plants, perennial grains were never domesticated. The solution to this challenge is to develop breeding pipelines that can  rapidly improve perennial grain crops. Our approach to this is to apply phenomic selection and genomic selection, individually or in combination, to realize rapid genetic gains in emerging perennial grains and oilseeds. 

Stage 2: Solution hypothesis confirmed: Work underway in this project and our previous FFAR award, as well as related work carried out by collaborators, have demonstrated that phenomic selection and genomic selection, carried out using genomic or phenomic data collected in early life stages (seeds and seedlings) can be used to successfully predict phenotypes of mature plants, and even in plants of the next generation. 

Stage 3: Undergoing lab/controlled research. At present we are working to determine if species that are at different stages of the domestication process (e.g., very earliest stages or more medium to advanced stages) require distinct breeding pipelines that use either phenomic or genomic selection or the two approaches in combination to expedite domestication. We are doing this by collecting genomic and phenomic data in the lab, and then planting out the plants from which the data were collected to monitor their phenotypes in the field over the course of three years. 

Stage 4: Undergoing research in a relevant environment. As described above, we are collected data from mature plants over the course of multiple years (e.g., growth and yield) and using these data to confirm whether or not phenomic selection, genomic selection and the combination of the two can be applied to realize genetic gains in emerging perennial grain and oilseed species. We are doing this both in a species that is relatively advanced in the domestication pipeline (Intermediate wheatgrass) and one that is fairly early on in the domestication pipeline (Silphium integrifolium).

column455: Some requested information, such as downstream use of project findings by industry, producers, or policymakers, can be difficult to gather during an active research project. In many cases, findings may influence early-stage discussions, material design decisions, or future planning, but they may not yet have resulted in formal products, policies, management recommendations, or extension outputs. It would be helpful if the reporting form allowed investigators to distinguish among confirmed use, preliminary use, potential use, and no known use.
column456:
Stage 1: Potential solution pathways identified ‚Äî 2 products

Two early-stage knowledge products were counted under Stage 1. The first is the identification of promising multiscale modeling strategies for understanding how molecular interactions, polymer miscibility, nanofiller arrangement, surface morphology, and interfacial properties affect the performance of bio-based polymer blends and nanocomposites. The intended use is to guide future experimental and computational design of sustainable packaging materials.

The second is the identification of AI/ML-enabled design pathways for polysaccharide-based nanocomposites. This includes the use of literature data, simulation results, and experimental data to develop future predictive models for processing‚Äìstructure‚Äìproperty relationships in sustainable packaging materials.

Stage 2: Solution hypothesis confirmed ‚Äî 2 products

Two knowledge products were counted under Stage 2. First, our atomistic and coarse-grained molecular dynamics studies confirmed the hypothesis that molecular-scale interactions, nanofiller organization, and surface features can be used to tune mechanical and viscoelastic properties of polymer nanocomposites. These findings support the use of multiscale modeling as a rational design tool for sustainable packaging materials.

Second, our AI/ML work confirmed the feasibility of using machine learning-based surrogate models to predict field responses in complex composite systems. This supports the hypothesis that ML tools can accelerate composite material design and reduce reliance on trial-and-error experimentation.

Stage 3: Undergoing lab/controlled research ‚Äî 3 products

Three products were counted under Stage 3. The first is the continued computational investigation of chitosan-containing polymer blends and nanocomposites using atomistic and coarse-grained molecular dynamics simulations. These studies remain in the controlled research stage and are intended to generate design rules for future material development.

The second is the development of an AI/ML-based field prediction framework for hierarchical and gradient composite systems. This tool is being evaluated in controlled computational settings and is intended to support rapid screening and optimization of composite designs.

The third is the ongoing experimental exploration of additional agricultural feedstocks, including corn stover-derived arabinoxylan and lignocellulose nanofibers, for potential use in sustainable packaging materials. These materials are still being evaluated at the laboratory stage.

Stage 4: Undergoing research in a relevant environment ‚Äî 1 product

One innovation was counted under Stage 4: the modified pearl millet starch-based coating for paper packaging developed in collaboration with Dr. Whiteside and collaborators. This material has moved beyond initial concept development and is being evaluated in packaging-relevant contexts, including assessment of moisture-barrier performance and compostability. The intended use is as a biodegradable and more sustainable alternative to conventional plastic or synthetic coatings for paper-based food packaging.

Stage 5: Undergoing research in an operational environment ‚Äî 1 product

One innovation was counted under Stage 5: the continued development of starch-based coatings and bioadhesive systems with consideration of practical processing, scalability, and industry relevance. Through engagement with BiologiQ, a company with expertise in starch-based and sustainable biomaterials, the project has begun to evaluate how these materials may perform under more application-oriented and industry-relevant conditions. This includes consideration of manufacturability, formulation requirements, and potential integration into paper-based packaging systems.

Stage 7: Product/solution fully validated and ready for market/user introduction ‚Äî 1 product

One product was counted under Stage 7: the crosslinked pearl millet starch-based coating formulation that demonstrated strong moisture-barrier performance, with a water vapor transmission rate of approximately 0.5 g/100 in¬≤/day, and biodegradation under composting conditions within approximately 2‚Äì3 weeks. These results indicate that the coating has reached a level of technical validation that supports further user or market-oriented evaluation. While additional scale-up, shelf-life, and commercialization studies are still needed, the material has demonstrated core functional performance relevant to sustainable paper-based food packaging applications.

Overall, the products reported here include both knowledge products, such as modeling frameworks, AI/ML tools, and peer-reviewed findings, and material innovations, such as starch-based coatings and bioadhesive systems. Together, these outputs support the project‚Äôs goal of developing sustainable, agriculture-derived packaging materials and design tools for food packaging applications.

The project will address research areas of reducing and redirecting food loss/waste and improving processing and packaging technologies. Specifically, we will demonstrate the effective design and fabrication of biopolymer-based nanocomposites for effective and sustainable food packaging, by integrating nanotechnological processing technology, multiscale computational modeling, and artificial intelligence.

The project will address research area of improving processing and packaging technologies and contributing to advancing UN Sustainable Development goals. Specifically, we will demonstrate the effective design and fabrication of biopolymer-based nanocomposites for effective and sustainable food packaging, by integrating nanotechnological processing technology, multiscale computational modeling, and artificial intelligence.

The overall goal of this consortium, guided by an advisory board consisting of US swine producers and allied industry partners, is to improve pig livability across all stages of production using these four pillars:
Pillar 1. Reduction in mortality causes on U.S. sow farms with the development and implementation of targeted strategies to maximize livability.
Pillar 2. Improve preweaning livability of piglets by impacting pre-farrow and post-farrowing strategies 
Pillar 3. Impact wean to finish livability through targeted management, nutrition, sow farm to market mortality modeling, and focusing labor efficiency 
Pillar 4. Expansion of the Nationally Recognized Extension and Outreach Program

column456:
A central Y1 priority has been the development of an accessible, standardized protocol for quantifying soil organic carbon (SOC) stocks at the field scale. This draft protocol is currently in Stage 5 (having passed through Stages 1 and 2). Unlike academic sampling campaigns, the protocol is written for non-academic practitioners ‚Äî Northeastern grazing producers, Technical Service Providers (TSPs) and technical-assistance (TA) staff, and other field practitioners ‚Äî who need defensible SOC estimates without specialized research infrastructure. The objective is to make rigorous methods usable, so that data collected across the region are internally consistent and comparable both over time and between operations.
The recommendations respond directly to a recurring problem: SOC results are frequently non-comparable because of small but consequential differences in how samples are collected, processed, and reported. The protocol therefore pairs plain-language guidance with a short set of non-negotiable methodological requirements. This protocol is being tested as part of summer soil sampling funded by the competitive small grants and will be presented at the RINGS Fall Convening in November for feedback.

column455:
Overall, the reporting process could be improved in some areas, particularly regarding the clarity of instructions and transparency of required data.
One of the main challenges is that it is not clear from the beginning that detailed information is required for every indicator reported. This becomes apparent only after starting to fill out the report, when additional fields and more granular data requests appear.
Given that this project involves a large, multi-institutional grant with several collaborators and subawardees, this lack of upfront clarity is especially challenging. It would be highly beneficial to have full visibility of all questions and required data elements at the outset of the reporting cycle. This would allow us to proactively share requirements with subawardees and collaborators, ensuring more consistent, complete, and efficient data collection across all partners.
To improve the process, I suggest the following:
‚Ä¢	Provide a complete list of all reporting questions and required details at the beginning of the reporting cycle
‚Ä¢	Identify ‚Äúcore required‚Äù versus ‚Äúoptional‚Äù or ‚Äúsupplemental‚Äù data to help prioritize effort
‚Ä¢	Offer a template or data collection framework that can be shared with collaborators in advance

column456: Solution Hypothesis Confirmed. Innovations from both objectives of the project are in the solution hypothesis confirmed stage. The innovation of supplementing donor cows with myoinositol is relevant to the cattle industry and bovine in vitro embryo production (IVP) industry specifically related to donor management and increasing production of high quality genetics. If our hypothesis is correct, cattle producers and IVP companies would be able to improve oocyte quality, number, and embryo development in donor cows by supplementing the cows directly with myoinositol in their feed. The innovation of supplementing oocyte maturation media with physiologically relevant levels of myoinositol is relevant to the cattle industry and bovine IVP industry specifically related to IVP procedures and increasing production of high quality genetics. If our hypothesis is correct, IVP companies will be able to improve embryo development by supplementing oocyte maturation media with myoinositol at the optimal concentration.

column456:
Stage 3: 
We identified a genetic regulator of survival under prolonged submergence in dormancy that likely has applicability to all aquatic plants based on our published comparative genomics study of the Lemna japonica hybrid group. 

We cloned multiple variants of the responsible gene from a Lemna japonica accession in our collection. 

Transgene constructs for overexpression were developed in a modular cloning system for ease of reconfiguration.

Stable transformants of Lemna japonica overexpressing the construct were recovered from tissue culture and displayed enhanced growth relative to controls, and an inability to survive the transition into dormancy.

Stage 4:
We have collected RNA-seq data from samples collected under simulated field conditions previously identified to induce high or low protein accumulation. These data are being used to develop markers to identify elite cultivars.
